era_context: Mid-3rd century BCE: the Hellenistic world continues to be shaped by Seleucid fragmentation and Ptolemaic dominance. Rome is consolidating Mediterranean power. Carthage is rebuilding through Iberian expansion. China's Han Dynasty is consolidating imperial authority.

events.title: Hamilcar Barca continues Carthaginian military campaigns in Iberia
events.description: Hamilcar Barca continues his military campaigns to expand Carthaginian control and influence in Iberia. These campaigns are part of a broader Carthaginian strategy to rebuild power and wealth following the First Punic War. Hamilcar's success in Iberia establishes a strong military and economic base that will be crucial for Carthage's future conflicts with Rome. The campaigns involve both conquest and the establishment of Carthaginian settlements and administrative structures.
events.certainty_note: Hamilcar's Iberian campaigns are well-attested in ancient sources, but the specific events of 238 BCE are not individually documented. The year falls within the traditionally dated period of his campaigns (c. 237–228 BCE).

events.title: Seleucid civil conflict persists; Antiochus Hierax maintains control of western territories
events.description: The Laodicean War between Seleucus II and Antiochus Hierax continues without resolution. Antiochus Hierax maintains control of western Anatolia and the Aegean regions, while Seleucus II controls the eastern portions of the empire. This bifurcation represents a fundamental weakening of Seleucid unity and power. The conflict allows Parthian expansion in the east and Egyptian encroachment in the south, further diminishing Seleucid territorial control.
events.certainty_note: The broader conflict is well-attested, but specific events in 238 BCE are not individually documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of Hamilcar Barca's Iberian campaigns remains uncertain. Some scholars argue that the traditional dating (c. 237–228 BCE) may be off by several years. Additionally, the degree of Carthaginian control in Iberia and the extent of indigenous resistance are debated among modern scholars.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 238 BCE is similar to previous years in this period: moderate but Hellenistic-centric. Specific events for this individual year are not well-documented. The historical record provides broader context about ongoing conflicts and political developments but not year-by-year detail. Carthaginian sources are entirely lost; our knowledge comes from Greek and Roman accounts written well after the events.
